label: Jakarta (Batavia)
sub: Capital city; established as Batavia by VOC in 1619, renamed Jakarta at independence. Population ~10M city, 34M metro. Sinking 25cm/year.

label: Nusantara (New Capital)
sub: Indonesia's designated new national capital in East Kalimantan. $32B project, Law No. 3/2022. Phase 1 government buildings partially operational 2024.

label: Yogyakarta
sub: Royal Sultanate city; Republican capital during independence war 1945–49. Diponegoro War base 1825–30. Cultural heart of Javanese civilization.

label: Borobudur
sub: World's largest Buddhist temple (c.800 AD), UNESCO World Heritage Site 1991. Built by Sailendra dynasty in central Java. Major Hindu-Buddhist heritage site.

label: Prambanan
sub: 9th-century Hindu temple compound, largest Hindu temple in Indonesia, UNESCO World Heritage Site. Dedicated to Trimurti (Brahma, Vishnu, Shiva).

label: Trowulan (Majapahit Capital)
sub: Site of the Majapahit Empire capital (1293–1527), East Java. Archaeological park with temples, royal baths, and the Bajang Ratu gate. UNESCO tentative list.

label: Surabaya
sub: Site of the Battle of Surabaya, November 10, 1945 — Indonesia's defining independence battle (Heroes' Day). Second largest city; major industrial and commercial hub.

label: Bandung
sub: Site of the 1955 Asian-African Bandung Conference, founding moment of the Non-Aligned Movement. Gedung Merdeka (Freedom Building) hosted 29 nations.

label: Palembang (Srivijaya)
sub: Capital of the Srivijaya Buddhist maritime empire (7th–13th centuries), controlling Strait of Malacca trade. Now South Sumatra's provincial capital.

label: Banda Aceh
sub: Capital of Aceh province; site of 29-year GAM independence conflict (1976–2005). Devastated by 2004 tsunami (130,000+ dead in Aceh). Helsinki Peace Accord 2005.

label: Jayapura (West Papua)
sub: Capital of Papua province (formerly Irian Jaya). Administered by Indonesia since 1963 New York Agreement. OPM independence movement ongoing. Papuan Special Autonomy since 2001.

label: Dili (Timor-Leste)
sub: Capital of Timor-Leste; occupied by Indonesia 1975–1999. Santa Cruz massacre 1991 (250+ killed). Independence referendum 1999 (78.5% for independence). Full independence May 20, 2002.

label: Ambon (Maluku)
sub: Site of the 1999–2002 Maluku communal violence (Muslim-Christian conflict), ~5,000 killed, 500,000 displaced. Former VOC spice trade center; Nutmeg Islands nearby.

label: Ternate (Spice Islands)
sub: Historical center of the global clove trade; the Sultanate of Ternate was one of the richest polities in pre-colonial Southeast Asia. Portuguese and later VOC battled for control of the Moluccas clove monopoly.

label: Makassar (Sulawesi)
sub: Capital of South Sulawesi; gateway to eastern Indonesia. Major port for nickel and agricultural exports. Site of the Sultanate of Gowa, historically the most powerful state in eastern Indonesia.

label: Medan (North Sumatra)
sub: Third largest city in Indonesia; economic capital of Sumatra. Major hub for palm oil, rubber, tobacco exports. Ethnically diverse: Batak, Malay, Chinese, Tamil communities.

label: Timika / Grasberg Mine
sub: Site of the Grasberg mine — world's largest gold mine and third-largest copper mine, operated by Freeport-McMoRan and PT Freeport Indonesia. Government acquired 51% stake in 2018. Revenues central to Papua development but also to corruption allegations.

label: Batam Island (Free Trade Zone)
sub: Indonesian island adjacent to Singapore, designated as a Free Trade Zone. Industrial manufacturing hub for electronics and shipbuilding. Part of the Indonesia-Malaysia-Singapore Growth Triangle.

label: Kuta, Bali (Bombings 2002)
sub: Site of the October 12, 2002 Bali bombings by Jemaah Islamiyah: 202 killed (88 Australian), the deadliest terrorist attack in Indonesian history. A second attack hit Jimbaran and Kuta on October 1, 2005, killing 23.

label: Madiun (PKI Affair 1948)
sub: Site of the 1948 PKI Madiun Affair, in which communist forces briefly declared a Soviet-backed government before being crushed by TNI forces loyal to Sukarno. The conflict created enduring Army-PKI enmity that exploded in the 1965 killings.

label: Tanjung Priok (1984 Massacre)
sub: Site of the September 12, 1984 Tanjung Priok massacre, in which security forces killed 24–100+ Muslim protesters. Indonesia's main port; critical to trade logistics.

label: Solo (Surakarta)
sub: Ancient Mataram Sultanate city, split by Dutch in 1755. Birthplace of Joko Widodo (Jokowi), 7th President. Center of Javanese batik and court culture. Solo Batik inscribed UNESCO Intangible Heritage.

label: Pontianak (West Kalimantan)
sub: Capital of West Kalimantan province, situated exactly on the equator. Major port city for Borneo agricultural exports. Site of ethnic violence between Madurese and Dayak communities in 1999–2001.

label: Manado (North Sulawesi)
sub: Capital of North Sulawesi province; predominantly Christian city, known for religious harmony. Gateway to Bunaken Marine Park. PERMESTA regional rebellion was based here in 1957.

label: Biak (West Papua)
sub: Strategic island in West Papua; site of the July 1998 Biak Massacre in which Indonesian security forces killed at least 100 Papuans who had raised the Morning Star flag. Major OPM (Free Papua Movement) activity.
